1 What is data rectangling?

  • Taking data that is fundamentally hierarchical, or tree-like, and converting it into a rectangular data frame made up of rows and columns.

  • This is important because hierarchical data is surprisingly common, especially when working with data that comes from the web.

  • lists make hierarchical data possible.

    • tidyr::unnest_longer() and tidyr::unnest_wider().

1.1 Packages to use

Code 
#install.packages("repurrrsive")
library(tidyverse)
library(repurrrsive)
library(jsonlite)

2 Lists

Code 
x1 <- list(1:4, "a", TRUE)
x1
[[1]]
[1] 1 2 3 4

[[2]]
[1] "a"

[[3]]
[1] TRUE

Naming the components, or children, of a list

Code 
x2 <- list(a = 1:2, b = 1:3, c = 1:4)
x2
$a
[1] 1 2

$b
[1] 1 2 3

$c
[1] 1 2 3 4

To print use str(), which generates a compact display of the structure, de-emphasizing the contents:

Code 
str(x1)
List of 3
 $ : int [1:4] 1 2 3 4
 $ : chr "a"
 $ : logi TRUE
Code 
str(x2)
List of 3
 $ a: int [1:2] 1 2
 $ b: int [1:3] 1 2 3
 $ c: int [1:4] 1 2 3 4

2.1 Hierarchy

  • Lists can contain any type of object, including other lists.
  • This makes them suitable for representing hierarchical (tree-like) structures:
Code 
x3 <- list(list(1, 2), list(3, 4))
str(x3)
List of 2
 $ :List of 2
  ..$ : num 1
  ..$ : num 2
 $ :List of 2
  ..$ : num 3
  ..$ : num 4
  • This is notably different to c(), which generates a flat vector:
Code 
c(c(1, 2), c(3, 4))
[1] 1 2 3 4
Code 
x5 <- list(1, list(2, list(3, list(4, list(5)))))
str(x5)
List of 2
 $ : num 1
 $ :List of 2
  ..$ : num 2
  ..$ :List of 2
  .. ..$ : num 3
  .. ..$ :List of 2
  .. .. ..$ : num 4
  .. .. ..$ :List of 1
  .. .. .. ..$ : num 5
Code 
view(x5)

2.2 List-Columns

Code 
df <- tibble(
  x = 1:2,
  y = c("a", "b"),
  z = list(list(1, 2), list(3, 4, 5))
)
df
Code 
df |>
  filter(x == 1)
  • Computing with list-columns is harder.
  • tibble() treats lists like vectors and the print method has been designed with lists in mind.

3 Unnesting

  • List-columns tend to come in two basic forms: named and unnamed.
  • When the children are named, they tend to have the same names in every row.

3.1 Named list-columns

  • Named list-columns naturally unnest into columns: each named element becomes a new named column.
Code 
df1 <- tribble(
  ~x , ~y                   ,
   1 , list(a = 11, b = 12) ,
   2 , list(a = 21, b = 22) ,
   3 , list(a = 31, b = 32) ,
)
df1

3.2 Unnamed list-columns

  • When the children are unnamed, the number of elements tends to vary from row-to-row.
  • Unnamed list-columns naturally unnest into rows: you’ll get one row for each child.
Code 
df2 <- tribble(
  ~x , ~y               ,
   1 , list(11, 12, 13) ,
   2 , list(21)         ,
   3 , list(31, 32)     ,
)

3.3 unnest_wider()

Used when each row has the same number of elements with the same names

Code 
df1 |>
  unnest_wider(y)

By default, the names of the new columns come exclusively from the names of the list elements, but you can use the names_sep argument to request that they combine the column name and the element name. This is useful for disambiguating repeated names.

Code 
df1 |>
  unnest_wider(y, names_sep = "_")

3.4 unnest_longer()

Used when each row contains an unnamed list

Code 
df2 |>
  unnest_longer(y)

Note how x is duplicated for each element inside of y: we get one row of output for each element inside the list-column.

But what happens if one of the elements is empty, as in the following example?

Code 
df6 <- tribble(
  ~x  , ~y         ,
  "a" , list(1, 2) ,
  "b" , list(3)    ,
  "c" , list()
)

df6 |>
  unnest_longer(y)

We get zero rows in the output, so the row effectively disappears.

If you want to preserve that row, adding NA in y, set keep_empty = TRUE.

Code 
df6 <- tribble(
  ~x  , ~y         ,
  "a" , list(1, 2) ,
  "b" , list(3)    ,
  "c" , list(NA)
)

df6 |>
  unnest_longer(y, keep_empty = TRUE)

3.5 Inconsitent Types

What happens if you unnest a list-column that contains different types of vector?

Code 
df4 <- tribble(
  ~x  , ~y                 ,
  "a" , list(1)            ,
  "b" , list("a", TRUE, 5)
)
Code 
df4 |>
  unnest_longer(y)

The output contains a list-column, but every element of the list-column contains a single element.

Dealing with inconsistent types is challenging and the details depend on the precise nature of the problem and your goals, but you’ll most likely need tools from Iteration.

3.6 Other functions from tidyr

  • unnest_auto() automatically picks between unnest_longer() and unnest_wider() based on the structure of the list-column. It’s great for rapid exploration, but ultimately it’s a bad idea because it doesn’t force you to understand how your data is structured, and makes your code harder to understand.

  • unnest() expands both rows and columns. It’s useful when you have a list-column that contains a 2d structure like a data frame, which you don’t see in this book, but you might encounter if you use the tidymodels ecosystem.

3.7 Exercises

  1. What happens when you use unnest_wider() with unnamed list-columns like df2? What argument is now necessary? What happens to missing values?

  2. What happens when you use unnest_longer() with named list-columns like df1? What additional information do you get in the output? How can you suppress that extra detail?

  3. From time-to-time you encounter data frames with multiple list-columns with aligned values. For example, in the following data frame, the values of y and z are aligned (i.e. y and z will always have the same length within a row, and the first value of y corresponds to the first value of z). What happens if you apply two unnest_longer() calls to this data frame? How can you preserve the relationship between x and y? (Hint: carefully read the docs).

Code 
df4 <- tribble(
  ~x  , ~y                              , ~z                              ,
  "a" , list("y-a-1", "y-a-2")          , list("z-a-1", "z-a-2")          ,
  "b" , list("y-b-1", "y-b-2", "y-b-3") , list("z-b-1", "z-b-2", "z-b-3")
)

4 Case Studies

Real data typically contains multiple levels of nesting that require multiple calls to unnest_longer() and/or unnest_wider().

4.1 Very wide data

gh_repos. This is a list that contains data about a collection of GitHub repositories retrieved using the GitHub API.

Code 
library(repurrrsive)
#view(gh_repos)

repos <- tibble(json = gh_repos)
repos
Code 
repos |>
  unnest_longer(json)
Code 
repos |>
  unnest_longer(json) |>
  unnest_wider(json)
Code 
repos |>
  unnest_longer(json) |>
  unnest_wider(json) |>
  names() |>
  head(10)
 [1] "id"          "name"        "full_name"   "owner"       "private"    
 [6] "html_url"    "description" "fork"        "url"         "forks_url"
Code 
repos |>
  unnest_longer(json) |>
  unnest_wider(json) |>
  select(id, full_name, owner, description)
Code 
repos |>
  unnest_longer(json) |>
  unnest_wider(json) |>
  select(id, full_name, owner, description) |>
  unnest_wider(owner)

Output:

Error in `unnest_wider()` at w09-hierarchical-data/ch23/draft-hierarchical-data-0.qmd:301:1:
! Can't duplicate names between the affected columns and the original data.
✖ These names are duplicated:
  ℹ `id`, from `owner`.
ℹ Use `names_sep` to disambiguate using the column name.
Code 
repos |>
  unnest_longer(json) |>
  unnest_wider(json) |>
  select(id, full_name, owner, description) |>
  unnest_wider(owner, names_sep = "_")

4.2 Relational data

Nested data is sometimes used to represent data that we’d usually spread across multiple data frames.

Example: got_chars which contains data about characters that appear in the Game of Thrones books and TV series.

Code 
chars <- tibble(json = got_chars)
chars
Code 
# Widening
chars |>
  unnest_wider(json)
Code 
# selecting a few
chars |>
  unnest_wider(json) |>
  select(id, name, gender, culture, born, died, alive)
Code 
# search for list-columns
chars |>
  unnest_wider(json) |>
  select(id, where(is.list))
Code 
# explore titles column
chars |>
  unnest_wider(json) |>
  select(id, titles) |>
  unnest_longer(titles)
Code 
# cleanning
titles <- chars |>
  unnest_wider(json) |>
  select(id, titles) |>
  unnest_longer(titles) |>
  filter(titles != "") |>
  rename(title = titles)

titles

You could imagine creating a table like this for each of the list-columns, then using joins to combine them with the character data as you need it.

4.3 Deeply nested

When the data is very deeply nested and requires repeated rounds of unnest_wider() and unnest_longer() to unravel: gmaps_cities.

Code 
gmaps_cities
Code 
gmaps_cities |>
  unnest_wider(json)
  • Drop the status column since they’re all OK
  • Unnamed list
Code 
gmaps_cities |>
  unnest_wider(json) |>
  select(-status) |>
  unnest_longer(results)

Now results is a named list, so we’ll use unnest_wider():

Code 
locations <- gmaps_cities |>
  unnest_wider(json) |>
  select(-status) |>
  unnest_longer(results) |>
  unnest_wider(results)

locations

There are a few different places we could go from here. We might want to determine the exact location of the match, which is stored in the geometry list-column:

Code 
locations |>
  select(city, formatted_address, geometry) |>
  unnest_wider(geometry)

We can unnest location to see the latitude (lat) and longitude (lng):

Code 
locations |>
  select(city, formatted_address, geometry) |>
  unnest_wider(geometry) |>
  unnest_wider(location)

Extracting the bounds requires a few more steps:

Code 
locations |>
  select(city, formatted_address, geometry) |>
  unnest_wider(geometry) |>
  # focus on the variables of interest
  select(!location:viewport) |>
  unnest_wider(bounds)

We then rename southwest and northeast (the corners of the rectangle) so we can use names_sep to create short but evocative names:

Code 
locations |>
  select(city, formatted_address, geometry) |>
  unnest_wider(geometry) |>
  select(!location:viewport) |>
  unnest_wider(bounds) |>
  rename(ne = northeast, sw = southwest) |>
  unnest_wider(c(ne, sw), names_sep = "_")

Once you’ve discovered the path to get to the components you’re interested in, you can extract them directly using another tidyr function, hoist():

Code 
locations |>
  select(city, formatted_address, geometry) |>
  hoist(
    geometry,
    ne_lat = c("bounds", "northeast", "lat"),
    sw_lat = c("bounds", "southwest", "lat"),
    ne_lng = c("bounds", "northeast", "lng"),
    sw_lng = c("bounds", "southwest", "lng"),
  )

4.4 What Is hoist()?

hoist() is a function from {tidyr} that lets you pull specific elements out of a list-column by name or position, without unnesting the entire structure. Think of it as a surgical tool compared to the blunt instruments of unnest_wider() and unnest_longer()

4.5 Exercises

  1. Roughly estimate when gh_repos was created. Why can you only roughly estimate the date?

  2. The owner column of gh_repos contains a lot of duplicated information because each owner can have many repos. Can you construct an owners data frame that contains one row for each owner? (Hint: does distinct() work with list-cols?)

  3. Follow the steps used for titles to create similar tables for the aliases, allegiances, books, and TV series for the Game of Thrones characters.

  4. Explain the following code line-by-line. Why is it interesting? Why does it work for got_chars but might not work in general?

Code 
tibble(json = got_chars) |>
  unnest_wider(json) |>
  select(id, where(is.list)) |>
  pivot_longer(
    where(is.list),
    names_to = "name",
    values_to = "value"
  ) |>
  unnest_longer(value)
  1. In gmaps_cities, what does address_components contain? Why does the length vary between rows? Unnest it appropriately to figure it out. (Hint: types always appears to contain two elements. Does unnest_wider() make it easier to work with than unnest_longer()?) .

5 JSON

JSON is short for javascript object notation and is the way that most web APIs return data. It’s important to understand it because while JSON and R’s data types are pretty similar, there isn’t a perfect 1-to-1 mapping, so it’s good to understand a bit about JSON if things go wrong.

5.1 Data Types

It has six key data types. Four of them are scalars:

  • The simplest type is a null (null) which plays the same role as NA in R. It represents the absence of data.

  • A string is much like a string in R, but must always use double quotes.

  • A number is similar to R’s numbers: they can use integer (e.g., 123), decimal (e.g., 123.45), or scientific (e.g., 1.23e3) notation. JSON doesn’t support Inf, -Inf, or NaN.

  • A boolean is similar to R’s TRUE and FALSE, but uses lowercase true and false.

To represent multiple values you need to use one of the two remaining types: arrays and objects.

  • Both arrays and objects are similar to lists in R; the difference is whether or not they’re named.

    • An array is like an unnamed list, and is written with []. For example [1, 2, 3] is an array containing 3 numbers, and [null, 1, "string", false] is an array that contains a null, a number, a string, and a boolean.

    • An object is like a named list, and is written with {}. The names (keys in JSON terminology) are strings, so must be surrounded by quotes. For example, {"x": 1, "y": 2} is an object that maps x to 1 and y to 2.

5.2 jsonlite

  • This package convert JSON into R data structures

  • read_json() and parse_json(). 

Code 
# A path to a json file inside the package:
gh_users_json()
[1] "C:/Users/jmjung/AppData/Local/Programs/R/R-4.5.2/library/repurrrsive/extdata/gh_users.json"
Code 
#> [1] "/home/runner/work/_temp/Library/repurrrsive/extdata/gh_users.json"

# Read it with read_json()
gh_users2 <- read_json(gh_users_json())

# Check it's the same as the data we were using previously
identical(gh_users, gh_users2)
[1] TRUE
Code 
#> [1] TRUE
Code 
str(parse_json("1"))
 int 1
Code 
str(parse_json('[1, 2, 3]')) # array
List of 3
 $ : int 1
 $ : int 2
 $ : int 3
Code 
str(parse_json('{"x": [1, 2, 3]}')) # object
List of 1
 $ x:List of 3
  ..$ : int 1
  ..$ : int 2
  ..$ : int 3

5.3 Starting the rectangling process

In most cases, JSON files contain a single top-level array

Code 
json <- '[
  {"name": "John", "age": 34},
  {"name": "Susan", "age": 27}
]'

df <- tibble(json = parse_json(json))
df
Code 
df |>
  unnest_wider(json)

In rarer cases, the JSON file consists of a single top-level JSON object, representing one “thing”. In this case, you’ll need to kick off the rectangling process by wrapping it in a list, before you put it in a tibble.

Code 
json <- '{
  "status": "OK", 
  "results": [
    {"name": "John", "age": 34},
    {"name": "Susan", "age": 27}
 ]
}
'
df <- tibble(json = list(parse_json(json)))
df
Code 
df |>
  unnest_wider(json) |>
  unnest_longer(results) |>
  unnest_wider(results)

Alternatively, you can reach inside the parsed JSON and start with the bit that you actually care about:

Code 
df <- tibble(results = parse_json(json)$results)
df |>
  unnest_wider(results)

6 Reference

# What is data rectangling?

-   Taking data that is fundamentally hierarchical, or tree-like, and converting it into a rectangular data frame made up of rows and columns.

-   This is important because hierarchical data is surprisingly common, especially when working with data that comes from the web.

-   lists make hierarchical data possible.

    -   `tidyr::unnest_longer()` and `tidyr::unnest_wider()`.

## Packages to use

```{r}
#install.packages("repurrrsive")
library(tidyverse)
library(repurrrsive)
library(jsonlite)
```

# Lists

```{r}
x1 <- list(1:4, "a", TRUE)
x1
```

> Naming the components, or children, of a list

```{r}
x2 <- list(a = 1:2, b = 1:3, c = 1:4)
x2
```

> To print use `str()`, which generates a compact display of the structure, de-emphasizing the contents:

```{r}
str(x1)

str(x2)
```

## Hierarchy

> -   Lists can contain any type of object, including other lists.
> -   This makes them suitable for representing hierarchical (tree-like) structures:

```{r}
x3 <- list(list(1, 2), list(3, 4))
str(x3)
```

> -   This is notably different to c(), which generates a flat vector:

```{r}
c(c(1, 2), c(3, 4))
```

```{r}
x5 <- list(1, list(2, list(3, list(4, list(5)))))
str(x5)

view(x5)
```

## List-Columns

```{r}
df <- tibble(
  x = 1:2,
  y = c("a", "b"),
  z = list(list(1, 2), list(3, 4, 5))
)
df
```

```{r}
df |>
  filter(x == 1)
```

> -   Computing with list-columns is harder.

> -   tibble() treats lists like vectors and the print method has been designed with lists in mind.

# Unnesting

> -   List-columns tend to come in two basic forms: named and unnamed.

> -   When the children are named, they tend to have the same names in every row.

## Named list-columns

> -   Named list-columns naturally unnest into **columns**: each named element becomes a new named column.

```{r}
df1 <- tribble(
  ~x , ~y                   ,
   1 , list(a = 11, b = 12) ,
   2 , list(a = 21, b = 22) ,
   3 , list(a = 31, b = 32) ,
)
df1
```

## Unnamed list-columns

> -   When the children are unnamed, the number of elements tends to vary from row-to-row.

> -   Unnamed list-columns naturally unnest into **rows**: you’ll get one row for each child.

```{r}
df2 <- tribble(
  ~x , ~y               ,
   1 , list(11, 12, 13) ,
   2 , list(21)         ,
   3 , list(31, 32)     ,
)
```

## `unnest_wider()`

> Used when each row has the same number of elements with the same names

```{r}
df1 |>
  unnest_wider(y)
```

> By default, the names of the new columns come exclusively from the names of the list elements, but you can use the `names_sep` argument to request that they combine the column name and the element name. This is useful for disambiguating repeated names.

```{r}
df1 |>
  unnest_wider(y, names_sep = "_")
```

## `unnest_longer()`

> Used when each row contains an unnamed list

```{r}
df2 |>
  unnest_longer(y)
```

> Note how x is duplicated for each element inside of y: we get one row of output for each element inside the list-column.

> But what happens if one of the elements is empty, as in the following example?

```{r}
df6 <- tribble(
  ~x  , ~y         ,
  "a" , list(1, 2) ,
  "b" , list(3)    ,
  "c" , list()
)

df6 |>
  unnest_longer(y)
```

> We get zero rows in the output, so the row effectively disappears.

> If you want to preserve that row, adding `NA` in y, set `keep_empty` = `TRUE`.

```{r}
df6 <- tribble(
  ~x  , ~y         ,
  "a" , list(1, 2) ,
  "b" , list(3)    ,
  "c" , list(NA)
)

df6 |>
  unnest_longer(y, keep_empty = TRUE)
```

## Inconsitent Types

> What happens if you unnest a list-column that contains different types of vector?

```{r}
df4 <- tribble(
  ~x  , ~y                 ,
  "a" , list(1)            ,
  "b" , list("a", TRUE, 5)
)
```

```{r}
df4 |>
  unnest_longer(y)
```

> The output contains a list-column, but every element of the list-column contains a single element.

> Dealing with inconsistent types is challenging and the details depend on the precise nature of the problem and your goals, but you’ll most likely need tools from Iteration.

## Other functions from tidyr

-   [`unnest_auto()`](https://tidyr.tidyverse.org/reference/unnest_auto.html) automatically picks between [`unnest_longer()`](https://tidyr.tidyverse.org/reference/unnest_longer.html) and [`unnest_wider()`](https://tidyr.tidyverse.org/reference/unnest_wider.html) based on the structure of the list-column. It’s great for rapid exploration, but ultimately it’s a bad idea because it doesn’t force you to understand how your data is structured, and makes your code harder to understand.

-   [`unnest()`](https://tidyr.tidyverse.org/reference/unnest.html) expands both rows and columns. It’s useful when you have a list-column that contains a **2d structure** like a data frame, which you don’t see in this book, but you might encounter if you use the [tidymodels](https://www.tmwr.org/base-r.html#combining-base-r-models-and-the-tidyverse) ecosystem.

## Exercises

1.  What happens when you use [`unnest_wider()`](https://tidyr.tidyverse.org/reference/unnest_wider.html) with unnamed list-columns like `df2`? What argument is now necessary? What happens to missing values?

2.  What happens when you use [`unnest_longer()`](https://tidyr.tidyverse.org/reference/unnest_longer.html) with named list-columns like `df1`? What additional information do you get in the output? How can you suppress that extra detail?

3.  From time-to-time you encounter data frames with multiple list-columns with aligned values. For example, in the following data frame, the values of `y` and `z` are aligned (i.e. `y` and `z` will always have the same length within a row, and the first value of `y` corresponds to the first value of `z`). What happens if you apply two [`unnest_longer()`](https://tidyr.tidyverse.org/reference/unnest_longer.html) calls to this data frame? How can you preserve the relationship between `x` and `y`? (Hint: carefully read the docs).

```{r}
df4 <- tribble(
  ~x  , ~y                              , ~z                              ,
  "a" , list("y-a-1", "y-a-2")          , list("z-a-1", "z-a-2")          ,
  "b" , list("y-b-1", "y-b-2", "y-b-3") , list("z-b-1", "z-b-2", "z-b-3")
)
```

# Case Studies

> Real data typically contains multiple levels of nesting that require multiple calls to unnest_longer() and/or unnest_wider().

## Very wide data

> **gh_repos**. This is a list that contains data about a collection of GitHub repositories retrieved using the GitHub API.

```{r}
library(repurrrsive)
#view(gh_repos)

repos <- tibble(json = gh_repos)
repos
```

```{r}
repos |>
  unnest_longer(json)
```

```{r}
repos |>
  unnest_longer(json) |>
  unnest_wider(json)

```

```{r}
repos |>
  unnest_longer(json) |>
  unnest_wider(json) |>
  names() |>
  head(10)
```

```{r}
repos |>
  unnest_longer(json) |>
  unnest_wider(json) |>
  select(id, full_name, owner, description)
```

```{r}
#| eval: false
repos |>
  unnest_longer(json) |>
  unnest_wider(json) |>
  select(id, full_name, owner, description) |>
  unnest_wider(owner)
```

**Output**:

```         
Error in `unnest_wider()` at w09-hierarchical-data/ch23/draft-hierarchical-data-0.qmd:301:1:
! Can't duplicate names between the affected columns and the original data.
✖ These names are duplicated:
  ℹ `id`, from `owner`.
ℹ Use `names_sep` to disambiguate using the column name.
```

```{r}
repos |>
  unnest_longer(json) |>
  unnest_wider(json) |>
  select(id, full_name, owner, description) |>
  unnest_wider(owner, names_sep = "_")
```

## Relational data

> Nested data is sometimes used to represent data that we’d usually spread across multiple data frames.

> **Example**: `got_chars` which contains data about characters that appear in the Game of Thrones books and TV series.

```{r}
chars <- tibble(json = got_chars)
chars

# Widening
chars |>
  unnest_wider(json)

# selecting a few
chars |>
  unnest_wider(json) |>
  select(id, name, gender, culture, born, died, alive)

# search for list-columns
chars |>
  unnest_wider(json) |>
  select(id, where(is.list))

# explore titles column
chars |>
  unnest_wider(json) |>
  select(id, titles) |>
  unnest_longer(titles)

# cleanning
titles <- chars |>
  unnest_wider(json) |>
  select(id, titles) |>
  unnest_longer(titles) |>
  filter(titles != "") |>
  rename(title = titles)

titles
```

> You could imagine creating a table like this for each of the list-columns, then using joins to combine them with the character data as you need it.

## Deeply nested

> When the data is very deeply nested and requires repeated rounds of `unnest_wider()` and `unnest_longer()` to unravel: `gmaps_cities`.

```{r}
gmaps_cities
```

```{r}
gmaps_cities |>
  unnest_wider(json)
```

> -   Drop the status column since they’re all OK

> -   Unnamed list

```{r}
gmaps_cities |>
  unnest_wider(json) |>
  select(-status) |>
  unnest_longer(results)
```

> Now results is a named list, so we’ll use `unnest_wider()`:

```{r}
locations <- gmaps_cities |>
  unnest_wider(json) |>
  select(-status) |>
  unnest_longer(results) |>
  unnest_wider(results)

locations
```

> There are a few different places we could go from here. We might want to determine the exact location of the match, which is stored in the `geometry` list-column:

```{r}
locations |>
  select(city, formatted_address, geometry) |>
  unnest_wider(geometry)
```

> We can unnest location to see the latitude (lat) and longitude (lng):

```{r}
locations |>
  select(city, formatted_address, geometry) |>
  unnest_wider(geometry) |>
  unnest_wider(location)
```

> Extracting the bounds requires a few more steps:

```{r}
locations |>
  select(city, formatted_address, geometry) |>
  unnest_wider(geometry) |>
  # focus on the variables of interest
  select(!location:viewport) |>
  unnest_wider(bounds)
```

> We then rename southwest and northeast (the corners of the rectangle) so we can use names_sep to create short but evocative names:

```{r}
locations |>
  select(city, formatted_address, geometry) |>
  unnest_wider(geometry) |>
  select(!location:viewport) |>
  unnest_wider(bounds) |>
  rename(ne = northeast, sw = southwest) |>
  unnest_wider(c(ne, sw), names_sep = "_")
```

> Once you’ve discovered the path to get to the components you’re interested in, you can extract them directly using another tidyr function, hoist():

```{r}
locations |>
  select(city, formatted_address, geometry) |>
  hoist(
    geometry,
    ne_lat = c("bounds", "northeast", "lat"),
    sw_lat = c("bounds", "southwest", "lat"),
    ne_lng = c("bounds", "northeast", "lng"),
    sw_lng = c("bounds", "southwest", "lng"),
  )
```

## What Is `hoist()`?

`hoist()` is a function from `{tidyr}` that lets you **pull specific elements out of a list-column by name or position**, without unnesting the entire structure. Think of it as a surgical tool compared to the blunt instruments of `unnest_wider()` and `unnest_longer()`

## Exercises

1.  Roughly estimate when `gh_repos` was created. Why can you only roughly estimate the date?

2.  The `owner` column of `gh_repos` contains a lot of duplicated information because each owner can have many repos. Can you construct an `owners` data frame that contains one row for each owner? (Hint: does [`distinct()`](https://dplyr.tidyverse.org/reference/distinct.html) work with `list-cols`?)

3.  Follow the steps used for `titles` to create similar tables for the aliases, allegiances, books, and TV series for the Game of Thrones characters.

4.  Explain the following code line-by-line. Why is it interesting? Why does it work for `got_chars` but might not work in general?

```{r}
tibble(json = got_chars) |>
  unnest_wider(json) |>
  select(id, where(is.list)) |>
  pivot_longer(
    where(is.list),
    names_to = "name",
    values_to = "value"
  ) |>
  unnest_longer(value)
```

5.  In `gmaps_cities`, what does `address_components` contain? Why does the length vary between rows? Unnest it appropriately to figure it out. (Hint: `types` always appears to contain two elements. Does [`unnest_wider()`](https://tidyr.tidyverse.org/reference/unnest_wider.html) make it easier to work with than [`unnest_longer()`](https://tidyr.tidyverse.org/reference/unnest_longer.html)?) .

# JSON

> JSON is short for **j**ava**s**cript **o**bject **n**otation and is the way that most web APIs return data. It’s important to understand it because while JSON and R’s data types are pretty similar, there isn’t a perfect 1-to-1 mapping, so it’s good to understand a bit about JSON if things go wrong.

## Data Types

It has six key data types. Four of them are scalars:

-   The simplest type is a null (`null`) which plays the same role as `NA` in R. It represents the absence of data.

-   A **string** is much like a string in R, but must always use double quotes.

-   A **number** is similar to R’s numbers: they can use integer (e.g., 123), decimal (e.g., 123.45), or scientific (e.g., 1.23e3) notation. JSON doesn’t support `Inf`, `-Inf`, or `NaN`.

-   A **boolean** is similar to R’s `TRUE` and `FALSE`, but uses lowercase `true` and `false`.

To represent multiple values you need to use one of the two remaining types: arrays and objects.

-   Both arrays and objects are similar to lists in R; the difference is whether or not they’re named.

    -   An **array** is like an unnamed list, and is written with `[]`. For example `[1, 2, 3]` is an array containing 3 numbers, and `[null, 1, "string", false]` is an array that contains a null, a number, a string, and a boolean.

    -   An **object** is like a named list, and is written with [`{}`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/Paren.html). The names (keys in JSON terminology) are strings, so must be surrounded by quotes. For example, `{"x": 1, "y": 2}` is an object that maps `x` to 1 and `y` to 2.

## jsonlite

-   This package convert JSON into R data structures

-   [`read_json()`](https://jeroen.r-universe.dev/jsonlite/reference/read_json.html) and [`parse_json()`](https://jeroen.r-universe.dev/jsonlite/reference/read_json.html). 

```{r}
# A path to a json file inside the package:
gh_users_json()
#> [1] "/home/runner/work/_temp/Library/repurrrsive/extdata/gh_users.json"

# Read it with read_json()
gh_users2 <- read_json(gh_users_json())

# Check it's the same as the data we were using previously
identical(gh_users, gh_users2)
#> [1] TRUE
```

```{r}
str(parse_json("1"))
```

```{r}
str(parse_json('[1, 2, 3]')) # array
```

```{r}
str(parse_json('{"x": [1, 2, 3]}')) # object
```

## Starting the rectangling process

> In most cases, JSON files contain a single top-level array

```{r}
json <- '[
  {"name": "John", "age": 34},
  {"name": "Susan", "age": 27}
]'

df <- tibble(json = parse_json(json))
df
```

```{r}
df |>
  unnest_wider(json)
```

> In rarer cases, the JSON file consists of a single top-level JSON object, representing one “thing”. In this case, you’ll need to kick off the rectangling process by wrapping it in a list, before you put it in a tibble.
```{r}
json <- '{
  "status": "OK", 
  "results": [
    {"name": "John", "age": 34},
    {"name": "Susan", "age": 27}
 ]
}
'
df <- tibble(json = list(parse_json(json)))
df
```

```{r}
df |>
  unnest_wider(json) |>
  unnest_longer(results) |>
  unnest_wider(results)
```

> Alternatively, you can reach inside the parsed JSON and start with the bit that you actually care about:

```{r}
df <- tibble(results = parse_json(json)$results)
df |>
  unnest_wider(results)
```

# Reference

[R for Data Science](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/rectangling.html)
